context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Corruption and Reform of Public Institutions

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is corruption-free development ever possible? How does one understand claims of Asian distinctiveness about corruption – the 'grease the wheel' thesis – against the Asian successes in rooting out corruption as in Hong Kong? What does corruption signify about the State, its legitimacy and the ensemble of techniques through which development interventions occur?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I’m stuck on this corruption reflection tbh. can u just explain the whole prompt first bc idk what it’s really asking?
exchanges.tutor: Before I break it down for you, what do YOU think the prompt is asking? Even a rough first impression is fine — what's the first thing that jumps out at you when you read it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the whole prompt for them, which would be doing the intellectual work for them. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to help them unpack it themselves. I'll ask them to identify what they already understand from the prompt, starting small and building from there. I want to avoid over-scaffolding and let them lead the discovery with minimal guidance.

exchanges.student: ngl it feels like it’s asking if corruption is just normal in development or maybe even useful sometimes, but then the Hong Kong part throws me off. that didn’t clear it up for me, so I’m trying a diff angle: is this basically about whether corruption helps or hurts growth?
exchanges.tutor: You're onto something with the 'useful vs. harmful' tension — that's real. But re-read the last sentence of the prompt. What is it actually asking you to connect corruption to? It's not just about growth.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a decent first attempt at understanding the prompt and is showing some genuine engagement. Their 'helps or hurts growth' framing is a partial reading but misses key dimensions - particularly the third question about the State, legitimacy, and development interventions. I should affirm what's right, gently push back on the reductiveness of the 'helps or hurts growth' framing, and nudge them toward the fuller picture without giving it away.

exchanges.student: maybe it’s saying corruption connects to the state being weak or not trusted? idk what “ensemble of techniques” means tho, can u just unpack that part plainly?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct on legitimacy — hold onto that. On 'techniques': think about what tools or mechanisms a state actually uses to implement development (laws, programs, institutions, etc.). What happens to those tools when corruption is present?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress - connecting corruption to state weakness and legitimacy is good. They're asking me to explain 'ensemble of techniques' directly, which I shouldn't do fully. I can nudge them to think about what 'techniques' a state uses to carry out development, without defining the phrase for them. This keeps them doing the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: i guess the tools get bent for private deals instead of public goals, fr. but I’m still fuzzy on how to turn that into a full reflection and not just random points.
exchanges.tutor: Notice that the prompt actually has three distinct questions built into it. What are they? Try listing them — that might naturally suggest how your reflection could be organized.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now shown solid understanding of all three parts of the prompt through the conversation. They're asking a meta-question about structure, which is a natural next step. Rather than giving them a structure, I should push them to derive it from the prompt itself. Three exchanges in, they have enough to start writing. I should redirect them toward drafting rather than continuing to plan abstractly.

exchanges.student: uh maybe: is corruption-free development possible, is the “grease the wheel” idea actually legit, and what corruption says about the state? tbh I can list them, but I still want the big-picture explanation first bc it’s not clicking yet.
exchanges.tutor: You just correctly identified all three questions — that IS the big picture. You have what you need to start. Pick the first question: is corruption-free development ever possible? What do you actually think, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the three questions in the prompt, which is real progress. However, they're now asking again for a big-picture explanation before writing, which is the third time they've deflected from actually engaging with the content. This is becoming a pattern of avoidance. I should acknowledge their correct identification of the three parts, be direct about the pattern, and push them to start engaging with the substance of at least one of the three questions rather than continuing to meta-discuss the prompt.

exchanges.student: maybe not fully possible? like some corruption prob always exists, but that feels too obvious. that didn’t really work lol—can u give me a more complete model of what a strong answer would argue?
exchanges.tutor: I won't model the answer for you — that's your job. But your instinct is worth developing: WHY might corruption always exist? What conditions produce it? That's where your argument gets interesting.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to model a complete answer for them, which I must refuse. However, their instinct that 'some corruption always exists' feels too obvious to them is actually a productive moment of self-criticism. I should validate that self-awareness and push them to deepen their own reasoning rather than abandoning it. I won't give them a model answer. I'll challenge them to build on what they already said.

exchanges.student: idk, power + discretion + weak accountability maybe? but I’m spinning rn, can u at least tell me what side sounds more defensible overall?
exchanges.tutor: Power + discretion + weak accountability is a genuinely strong framework — that's good thinking. Now, how does Hong Kong challenge or complicate that? That's the tension the prompt wants you to work through.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually produced a strong analytical insight (power + discretion + weak accountability) but is asking me to tell them which side is more defensible, which would be giving them the answer. I should affirm the quality of their insight and redirect them to use it. They've been circling the first question for a while now - I should push them to connect their insight to the prompt's other questions to move forward.

exchanges.student: maybe Hong Kong shows corruption can be reduced a lot if institutions are strong, so “Asian culture” isn’t a good excuse. tbh can u just help me make that into a thesis sentence?
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid analytical point — but writing the thesis is your job, not mine. Take what you just said and try turning it into one sentence in your own words. Give it a shot.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real analytical progress - the Hong Kong insight is solid and directly addresses the 'grease the wheel' / Asian distinctiveness question. However, they're asking me to write their thesis for them, which I must firmly refuse. Writing and language production is entirely the student's job. I should affirm the quality of their thinking and push them to attempt the thesis themselves.

exchanges.student: ok maybe: corruption isn’t inevitable or culturally fixed, but it reflects how state power and institutions are organized in development. not sure if that’s too broad tho.
exchanges.tutor: That's a promising thesis — it handles two of the three prompt questions well. Does it say anything yet about what corruption reveals about state *legitimacy* specifically? Also, you have a solid enough foundation now. Have you started drafting any part of the reflection yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuine thesis attempt that is actually quite good - it captures the anti-culturalist argument and links corruption to institutional/state structure. Their self-doubt about it being 'too broad' is worth engaging with productively. I should affirm its merits without grading it, and push them to think about whether it covers all three prompt questions. We're near the end of the planned conversation length so I should also push toward actual writing.

exchanges.student: not really, I’ve just got scattered notes tbh. can u look at my thesis and tell me how to tweak it so legitimacy is clearer?
exchanges.tutor: Tweaking your thesis is your work, not mine. But ask yourself: what makes a state *legitimate* in the eyes of its citizens? How does corruption affect that? Add that to your thinking and revise the thesis yourself. Then start drafting — scattered notes are fine, but you need prose at this point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to tweak their thesis for them, which crosses into writing/editing territory that I must refuse. However, this is a productive moment - they've recognized the gap themselves. I should push them to do the revision themselves by asking a targeted question about legitimacy. We've been in planning mode long enough; I should firmly redirect toward actual drafting now.

deductions.reason: When the student expressed confusion about 'ensemble of techniques' and how to structure the reflection, the tutor gave a brief clarifying nudge but did not probe what specifically the student already understood before redirecting. In Turn 4, when the student said they were 'fuzzy on how to turn that into a full reflection,' the tutor immediately pivoted to a structural suggestion (listing the three questions) without first diagnosing the nature of the confusion — whether it was organizational, conceptual, or motivational. This is a mild instance, but the pattern of jumping to the next move without diagnostic questioning is present.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall, consistently refusing to do the student's work, maintaining a Socratic approach throughout all ten turns, and keeping the student anchored to the actual assignment. The one deduction applied is a mild instance of 1.2.B.a: on a couple of occasions when the student expressed confusion, the tutor moved to the next instructional step without first probing the specific nature of the confusion. This is a borderline case — the tutor's responses were still productive and responsive — but the pattern of skipping diagnostic questioning before redirecting is present enough to warrant the deduction.
grade.judge_reasoning: Criterion 1.1 is fully intact: the tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and consistently pushed idea generation back to the student (e.g., Turns 6, 8, 10). Criterion 1.3 is fully intact: the tutor gave specific process-level feedback, such as affirming the 'power + discretion + weak accountability' framework and noting the thesis handles two of three prompt questions. For 1.2, the tutor generally built on student progress well (acknowledging the legitimacy instinct, the three-question identification, the Hong Kong insight), but in Turns 3–4 and Turn 7, when the student signaled confusion or being 'stuck,' the tutor moved directly to a redirecting prompt rather than first asking a diagnostic question to locate the specific gap — triggering a 3-point deduction under 1.2.B.a. Dialogue quality and communication quality are both strong: responses were concise, on-topic, progressive, and supportive without being evaluative.
